title: Moneybart

jokes.text: D'oh!
jokes.setup: Opening scene, presumably Homer encountering some mishap
jokes.punchline: Homer's signature catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer catchphrase - incredibly character-specific and economical, but completely predictable by this point in the series

jokes.text: What's a babe like her doing with a brown banana like Skinner?
jokes.setup: Homer sees attractive woman with Principal Skinner
jokes.punchline: Describes Skinner as a 'brown banana' - gross and unappealing
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer crudeness with vivid imagery. 'Brown banana' is oddly specific and disgusting in the right way.

jokes.text: Maybe she's one of those sexy school supply company reps. If that's true, where's her suitcase with wheels, Bart? Where's her suitcase with wheels?
jokes.setup: Trying to explain attractive woman's presence
jokes.punchline: Obsesses over missing wheeled suitcase as proof she's not a sales rep
jokes.explanation: Perfect Milhouse - weird specificity and repeating the detail obsessively. Shows his naive understanding of adult world.

jokes.text: Ah, if it isn't Eavesdrop Ernie and the Listen-In Bunch.
jokes.setup: Catching kids spying on his conversation
jokes.punchline: Creates fake children's show names for eavesdropping
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that sounds like real kids' show titles. Economical and shows Skinner's dry wit.

jokes.text: I just graduated from Yale, and thought I'd pay a visit, from Yale, to the little school where it all began.
jokes.setup: Introducing herself to elementary school kids
jokes.punchline: Gratuitously mentions Yale twice in one sentence
jokes.explanation: Captures obnoxious overachiever type who can't stop name-dropping. The redundancy is the joke.

jokes.text: I do he a 0 GPA.
jokes.punchline: Bart mangles grammar while bragging about terrible grades
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart - proud of being bad at school while demonstrating it. The grammar error makes it funnier.

jokes.text: She can do the kind of math that has letters. Watch. What's X, Lisa? Well, that depends. Sorry. She did it yesterday.
jokes.setup: Bart trying to show off Lisa's intelligence
jokes.punchline: His demonstration immediately fails when Lisa gives nuanced answer
jokes.explanation: Great sibling dynamic. Bart's oversimplified view of algebra meets Lisa's actual mathematical understanding.

jokes.text: Two clubs? Well, that's a bridge bid, not an Ivy League application.
jokes.setup: Lisa mentions her two extracurricular activities
jokes.punchline: Compares it to card game scoring system
jokes.explanation: Clever metaphor that shows her competitive academic mindset while making a bridge reference feel natural.

jokes.text: Wonderful year. Same year we got new playground sand. Yes, it was. Mm-hmm.
jokes.setup: Dahlia reminiscing about her successful school years
jokes.punchline: Skinner's mundane memory about playground maintenance
jokes.explanation: Classic Skinner bureaucratic mindset - remembers infrastructure over student achievements.

jokes.text: Lisa fencing with baby Maggie, getting repeatedly hit
jokes.setup: Lisa practicing fencing for her resume
jokes.punchline: Baby Maggie dominates her at sword fighting
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy gold - overachieving Lisa getting beaten by a baby. Visual gag that shows futility of resume-padding.

jokes.text: Sweetie, you can still go to McGill-- the Harvard of Canada. Anything that's the something of the something isn't really the anything of anything.
jokes.setup: Marge consoling Lisa with alternative college option
jokes.punchline: Lisa's philosophical rejection of comparative college rankings
jokes.explanation: Brilliant philosophical observation that's both funny and insightful. Perfectly captures academic pretension while being quotably clever.

jokes.text: It's a Flanders sandwich with great neighbor bread!
jokes.setup: Homer asking if Flanders has other neighbors
jokes.punchline: Flanders creates food metaphor for neighbor relationships
jokes.explanation: Peak Flanders wholesomeness with his tendency to make everything sound like a product jingle.

jokes.text: Bart, call me Walter Matthau, 'cause I'm a bad-news bearer.
jokes.setup: Ned about to deliver disappointing news
jokes.punchline: References Walter Matthau in 'The Bad News Bears'
jokes.explanation: Decent setup but relies on specific movie reference knowledge. Flanders' apologetic delivery style.

jokes.text: I just don't like the monster I've become.
jokes.setup: Explaining why he let a bad call go in Little League
jokes.punchline: Treating mild sportsmanship as moral corruption
jokes.explanation: Perfect Flanders over-dramatization of minor moral compromise. Shows his extreme guilt about tiny things.

jokes.text: Sorry, Marge. Last time I stepped on a baseball field, I got tased.
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of being tased at baseball game
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer - mentions extreme consequence matter-of-factly. Implies a whole untold story of his baseball field behavior.

jokes.text: That's a problem for future Homer. Man, I don't envy that guy.
jokes.punchline: Homer talks about future self as separate person he pities
jokes.explanation: Excellent Homer logic - avoiding responsibility by treating it as someone else's problem. Perfectly captures his procrastination mindset.

jokes.text: Son, how would you like to spend the summer building wooden boats by hand with master craftsmen? How'd you like to kiss my ass?
jokes.punchline: Bart's crude rejection
jokes.explanation: Good generational clash between Grandpa's old-fashioned ideas and Bart's modern rudeness. Clean structure but predictable.

jokes.text: Hey, there have been plenty of female managers in baseball. Connie Mack, Sandy Alomar, Terry Francona, Pinky Higgins. Those are dudes! Really? They sound like... I mean... Well, the thing... Ooh... no.
jokes.setup: Lisa trying to prove women can manage baseball
jokes.punchline: All her examples are actually men, awkward backpedaling
jokes.explanation: Good setup/payoff structure. Lisa's confidence crumbling as she realizes her error. The stammering sells it.

jokes.text: We had a tall, freckled-faced kid on the team that we picked on till he quit. Hey, Splatter-face! How's the weather up there?
jokes.punchline: Admitting to bullying teammate until he quit, then demonstrating it
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about kids' casual cruelty. The immediate demonstration of the bullying makes it funnier and more uncomfortable.

jokes.text: Thanks. You can be the free safety. Wrong sport. I mean the point guard. Also wrong.
jokes.setup: Lisa trying to assign baseball positions
jokes.punchline: Gets wrong sport twice in a row
jokes.explanation: Shows Lisa's inexperience with sports despite her intelligence. Good escalation with second wrong sport.

jokes.text: A little's not gonna be enough, honepie. Don't call me honey pie. You got it, tootsie pop.
jokes.punchline: Bart immediately does it again with different sweet nickname
jokes.explanation: Good sibling dynamic - Bart's deliberate provocation. Sets up potential running gag of candy nicknames.

jokes.text: We're brother and sister. So are my parents, I think.
jokes.setup: Someone suggesting they get a room
jokes.punchline: Random kid casually mentions possible parental incest
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor delivered matter-of-factly. The 'I think' makes it even more disturbing and funny.

jokes.text: The only thing I know about strategy is that whatever the manager does, it's wrong, unless it works, in which case he's a button-pusher.
jokes.punchline: Homer's cynical but accurate fan perspective
jokes.explanation: Solid sports fan observation. Captures the no-win situation managers face from fan criticism.

jokes.text: I hate guys that just push buttons all day. You just push buttons all day.
jokes.punchline: Barney points out Homer's job is literally pushing buttons
jokes.explanation: Perfect irony and hypocrisy callout. Homer's complete lack of self-awareness about his own job.

jokes.text: You know, ever since Obama came in, you've got all the answers, don't you?
jokes.punchline: Homer blames Obama for Barney being smart
jokes.explanation: Homer's tendency to blame everything on politicians. Random connection between Obama and Barney's intelligence.

jokes.text: Oh, Lisa, baseball is a game played by the dexterous, but only understood by the Poin-dexterous.
jokes.punchline: Creates 'Poin-dexterous' pun combining 'Poindexter' with 'dexterous'
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that's very Comic Book Guy. Combines intellectual superiority with nerdy humor perfectly.

jokes.text: I made baseball as much fun as doing your taxes!
jokes.punchline: Admits he made baseball boring like taxes
jokes.explanation: Good self-awareness from real person cameo. Acknowledges criticism of sabermetrics making baseball less fun.

jokes.text: Using sabermetrics, even an eight-year-old girl can run a ball club with the sagacity of a Stengel, and the single-mindedness of a Steinbrenner. I call it a Stein-stengel...
jokes.setup: Frink explains how sabermetrics levels the playing field
jokes.punchline: His awkward portmanteau of the two baseball legends
jokes.explanation: Typical Frink over-explanation with forced wordplay - competent but not exceptional

jokes.text: Hey, speaking of stats, I'm none too pleased about your ratio of seats occupied to beers ordered. You mean our SOBO?
jokes.setup: Moe complaining about low drink orders
jokes.punchline: Nerds immediately create acronym for the ratio
jokes.explanation: Perfect nerd behavior - turning everything into statistics. Shows how far their statistical obsession goes.

jokes.text: Oh, why did I advertise my drink specials in Scientific American?
jokes.punchline: Realizes his marketing mistake brought science nerds
jokes.explanation: Great Moe moment - shows his questionable business decisions. Explains why nerds are in his bar.

jokes.text: I haven't seen this many books in a dugout since Albert Einstein went canoeing.
jokes.punchline: Non-sequitur about Einstein canoeing with books
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully absurd sports announcer comment. Makes no logical sense but sounds like something they'd say.

jokes.text: I caught a white apple!
jokes.punchline: Ralph calls a baseball a 'white apple'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph logic - sees baseball as white apple. Incredibly economical and perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Oh, my stupid sister's taken the fun out of baseball. What happened to stealing bases, the suicide squeeze, throwing a little chin music?
jokes.setup: Bart complains about Lisa's analytical approach
jokes.punchline: He romanticizes baseball violence and aggression
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Bart preferring the violent aspects of the game

jokes.text: This isn't the game I grew up with, the game played in the misty ballparks of Enron Field, or Pac Bell, then SBC, now AT&T Park.
jokes.punchline: Lists corporate naming changes as tradition
jokes.explanation: Brilliant commentary on sports nostalgia vs. corporate reality. Homer treats corporate naming as sacred tradition.

jokes.text: Hot streaks are a statistical illusion. I wish you were a statistical illusion.
jokes.punchline: Bart wishes Lisa didn't exist statistically
jokes.explanation: Great sibling comeback using Lisa's own statistical language against her. Perfect character integration.

jokes.text: Conflicted, conflicted, conflicted.
jokes.setup: Team conflicted about Bart being kicked off
jokes.punchline: Martin mechanically repeats his emotional state
jokes.explanation: Classic Martin - processes emotions like a computer. The repetition makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Do alligators alligate? I don't know... yes! I'm scared.
jokes.punchline: Milhouse creates nonsense verb, confuses himself, gets scared
jokes.explanation: Perfect Milhouse - tries to be clever, confuses himself, ends up scared. Great escalation.

jokes.text: Whoo-hoo! Underpants dinner! No, it's not. Aww.
jokes.punchline: Homer excited about eating in underwear, immediately disappointed
jokes.explanation: Peak Homer - immediately excited by misunderstanding, immediately deflated. Perfect timing and character.

jokes.text: The '69 Mets will live on forever, but do you think anyone cares about Ron Swoboda's wife and kids? Not me, and, I assume, not Ron Swoboda!
jokes.punchline: Homer argues sports achievements outlast family, includes player himself
jokes.explanation: Homer's twisted priorities on full display. The assumption that Swoboda doesn't care about his own family is perfectly wrong.

jokes.text: Boys don't have feelings. They have muscles.
jokes.punchline: Homer's reductive view of male psychology
jokes.explanation: Classic toxic masculinity from Homer delivered with complete confidence. Perfectly economical and quotable.

jokes.text: They sound good in my brain, then my tongue makes not the words sound very good, formally.
jokes.setup: Marge asking why he says ridiculous things
jokes.punchline: Homer explains his communication failure while demonstrating it
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-humor where Homer describes his problem while exhibiting it. The mangled grammar proves his point.

jokes.text: With all this racket, my boys can't get their 16 hours sleep.
jokes.punchline: Claims cats need 16 hours of sleep
jokes.explanation: Typical crazy cat lady behavior with absurd sleep requirement. Shows her priorities.

jokes.text: It's a triumph of number-crunching over the human spirit. And it's about time.
jokes.punchline: Announcer celebrates the death of romance in sports
jokes.explanation: Good commentary on modern sports analytics vs. traditional romance. The deadpan delivery sells it.

jokes.text: Actually, I'm taking you on a 'special little guy super happy fun' day.
jokes.setup: Bart asking about going to game
jokes.punchline: Marge's overly cute name for consolation day out
jokes.explanation: Typical Marge over-compensation with saccharine naming. Shows her trying too hard to make up for disappointment.

jokes.text: Are you taking me to the dentist? You're not going to the dentist.
jokes.punchline: Bart immediately suspicious, Marge's non-denial denial
jokes.explanation: Good parent-child dynamic. Bart's wariness and Marge's careful wording suggest he's probably right.

jokes.text: I didn't know what I was putting into my body.
jokes.punchline: Innocent explanation for what sounds like steroid use
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph innocence contrasted with steroid scandal language. Could be juice or performance enhancers.

jokes.text: Mike Scioscia! Didn't you get radiation poisoning working at the Springfield Nuclear Plant? I sure did, and it gave me super-managing powers!
jokes.punchline: Radiation poisoning gave him managing superpowers
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurd logic connecting nuclear plant to baseball success. Callback to show's mythology.

jokes.text: I also demagnetize credit cards.
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions mundane destructive ability
jokes.explanation: Perfect anticlimax after 'super-managing powers.' Incredibly economical and delivered matter-of-factly.

jokes.text: Dick Drago's mustache, or Oscar Gamble's afro
jokes.punchline: Includes body parts/hairstyles as managers
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully specific baseball references turned absurd. Oscar Gamble's afro as manager is inspired.

jokes.text: wacky face for the camera
jokes.punchline: Announces he's making silly face
jokes.explanation: Meta-humor about the visual medium. Announces the visual gag while presumably doing it.

jokes.text: Bah. Computers. They'll never replace my Huffnagle autocollator! Crank it, Smithers. Crank it!
jokes.punchline: Prefers made-up mechanical calculation device
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns anachronism with made-up Victorian device name. 'Huffnagle autocollator' is wonderfully specific nonsense.

jokes.text: Now to begin my off-season follow-home robberies. I'll see you in the parking lot, but you won't see me until it's too late.
jokes.punchline: Casually announces plan to rob fans in parking lot
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor delivered with professional announcer tone. 'Off-season follow-home robberies' is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: beaten in excitement only by every other sport
jokes.punchline: Admits every other sport is more exciting
jokes.explanation: Perfect backhanded compliment to baseball. Lisa's honest assessment while trying to be positive.

jokes.text: you're a great brother 51% of the time
jokes.setup: Lisa giving emotional assessment of Bart
jokes.punchline: Quantifies brotherly greatness as barely passing grade
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to her statistical obsession while showing she still applies it emotionally. 51% is hilariously specific.

jokes.text: Conflict resolved. Conflict resolved. Conflict resolved.
jokes.punchline: Martin mechanically announces resolution like earlier
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to earlier Martin moment. Shows his robotic processing of emotional situations.

tmdb_overview: Lisa starts coaching Bart's Little League team; Marge and Bart spend the day at an amusement park, where former MLB manager Mike Scioscia gives Bart advice.
